app.name: OpenAI Deep Research
app.bestFor: Best overall for breadth and report quality — runs a multi-step autonomous loop reading full pages and citing sources.
app.rankBasis: Ranked at or near the top in all three cited 2026 deep-research comparisons, with the strongest consensus for broad autonomous browsing and polished cited reports.
app.pricingFree: Free ChatGPT includes limited lightweight deep-research usage
app.pricingPaid: ChatGPT Plus $20/month — expanded deep-research access; higher tiers provide larger allowances
pros: Comparisons consistently praise report breadth and structure
Handles open-ended research plans with little manual orchestration
Citations make source checking easier than ordinary chat answers
cons: Full runs can take several minutes and consume limited monthly allowances
Citation presence does not guarantee that every claim is supported correctly
Web access and source availability can leave important paywalled material uncovered

app.name: Perplexity (Deep Research)
app.bestFor: Fast citation-backed answers with a generous free tier — the go-to for quick sourced web research.
app.rankBasis: Appears in all three cited deep-research comparisons and is repeatedly recommended as the fastest mainstream option for citation-backed web answers.
app.pricingFree: Free plan includes a limited number of Research queries and standard searches
app.pricingPaid: Perplexity Pro $20/month — higher Research limits, premium models, and file analysis
pros: Reviewers consistently value speed and visible inline citations
Low-friction interface works well for iterative fact finding
Broad search coverage is useful for current web topics
cons: Answers can over-rely on secondary sources or misread cited pages
Research limits apply even on paid plans
Long-form synthesis is often less nuanced than slower specialist workflows

app.name: Claude Research
app.bestFor: Best for analytical depth and reasoning over sources when you want careful synthesis over sheer breadth.
app.rankBasis: Placed among the leaders in all three cited 2026 comparisons, with reviewers particularly distinguishing its careful source synthesis and analytical writing.
app.pricingFree: Free Claude plan does not provide a dependable standing Research allowance
app.pricingPaid: Claude Pro $20/month — Research access with usage limits; Max and team tiers provide higher capacity
pros: Comparisons praise nuanced reasoning and readable synthesis
Strong at reconciling and explaining conflicting source material
Large context helps when research includes user-provided documents
cons: Research availability and limits vary by plan and region
Lower paid tiers can hit usage caps during intensive work
Coverage may be narrower than search-first tools on fast-moving topics

app.name: Gemini Deep Research
app.bestFor: Strong long-form research reports with a meaningful free tier and deep Google ecosystem integration.
app.rankBasis: Included in all three cited deep-research roundups and stands out for combining broad web research with Google's search and Workspace ecosystem.
app.pricingFree: Free Gemini plan includes limited Deep Research access
app.pricingPaid: Google AI Pro $19.99/month — higher Deep Research limits plus expanded Gemini and NotebookLM access
pros: Reviewers value the substantial report depth available at low entry cost
Research plans can be inspected and adjusted before execution
Strong integration with Google documents and services
cons: Reports can be verbose and repetitive
Source quality still varies despite Google Search grounding
Feature limits and model availability change across regions and account types

app.name: Elicit
app.bestFor: Academic literature review across millions of papers — extraction and screening for systematic reviews.
app.rankBasis: Appears in all three cited academic-research comparisons and has a strong specialist reputation for structured literature screening and evidence extraction rather than general web search.
app.pricingFree: Free Basic plan with limited automated reports and workflow credits
app.pricingPaid: Plus from $12/month when billed annually — larger report, extraction, and screening allowances
pros: Researchers value the structured extraction tables and screening workflow
More reproducible for literature review than general chat assistants
Links claims back to papers and extracted passages
cons: Best coverage is scholarly literature, not the open web
Automated extraction still needs domain-expert verification
Meaningful systematic-review workloads can exhaust lower-tier credits

app.name: Consensus
app.bestFor: Evidence-based answers to factual research questions, pulling directly from peer-reviewed studies.
app.rankBasis: Recommended in both cited research-assistant roundups and differentiated by a purpose-built corpus of peer-reviewed papers plus evidence-oriented answer summaries.
app.pricingFree: Free plan with limited Pro analyses and Deep Searches
app.pricingPaid: Premium plans available with higher Deep Search and analysis limits; current price depends on billing cadence
pros: Users value fast answers grounded in peer-reviewed studies
Consensus summaries help expose whether evidence is mixed
Much easier than constructing equivalent database queries manually
cons: A simplified consensus label can hide study-design differences
Coverage depends on indexed papers and accessible abstracts or full text
Not designed for broad market, news, or web research

app.name: NotebookLM
app.bestFor: Research grounded in your own uploaded documents — Q&A and audio overviews over a private source set.
app.rankBasis: Included in both cited general-research comparisons and has become the default source-grounded notebook for Google users, although it researches a supplied corpus rather than the whole web.
app.pricingFree: Free plan supports up to 100 notebooks, 50 sources per notebook, and daily chat and Audio Overview limits
app.pricingPaid: Google AI Pro $19.99/month — substantially higher notebook, source, chat, and Audio Overview limits
pros: Users praise accurate navigation back to the supplied source passage
Audio Overviews make large source sets easier to consume
Free tier is generous for personal research projects
cons: Does not replace open-web discovery because answers are bounded by imported sources
Source ingestion and output limits still constrain large corpora
Generated audio and summaries can flatten nuance or omit details

skill.name: last30days
skill.bestFor: The most-starred research skill (53.4k★, MIT, reached #1 on GitHub Trending) — sweeps Reddit, X, YouTube, HN, and the web for a topic and synthesizes a grounded, cited summary.
skill.rankBasis: The cited GitHub source records roughly 53.4k stars and a #1 GitHub Trending run, making it the clearest adoption leader among standalone recency-research skills.
features: Reddit, X, YouTube, Hacker News, and web coverage
pros: Strong community adoption for a narrowly defined research job
Combines community discussion with conventional web sources
Transparent skill workflow is easy to inspect and adapt
cons: Some sources require external credentials, cookies, or changing integrations
Recency focus is a poor fit for historical or foundational research
Cross-platform scraping can break when upstream sites change

skill.name: imbad0202/academic-research-skills
skill.bestFor: A full research -> write -> review -> revise pipeline skill (39.3k★) with a multi-agent research team, systematic-review modes, and citation verification.
skill.rankBasis: The existing GitHub source records roughly 39.3k stars, an unusually strong signal for an academic agent-skill collection and second only to last30days in this track.
pros: Covers the full research-to-writing loop rather than a single search step
Explicit review and citation stages encourage more disciplined output
Large community signal for a specialist skill repository
cons: Complex multi-agent runs require substantial model tokens and setup
Output quality depends on the chosen models and search tools
Automated citation checks do not replace scholarly review

skill.name: GPT Researcher
skill.bestFor: The recognized open-source deep-research agent (28k★) — plans, searches, reads many sources, and writes a long, cited report; ships as a Python package and full web app.
skill.rankBasis: The cited GitHub source records roughly 28k stars, and independent open-source agent roundups consistently treat GPT Researcher as the reference implementation for autonomous cited reports.
skill.pricingPaid: Optional hosted service and external model/search APIs may incur usage charges
pros: Mature open-source project with many integrations and deployment paths
Produces structured reports with traceable sources
Can be customized for private or specialist research workflows
cons: Self-hosting requires API keys, infrastructure, and cost controls
Long research runs can consume substantial model and search credits
Source selection and citation accuracy still require review

skill.name: STORM (Stanford OVAL)
skill.bestFor: Stanford's LLM knowledge-curation system (30.3k★) — researches a topic and generates a full-length, Wikipedia-style report with citations; Co-STORM adds human-in-the-loop.
skill.rankBasis: The cited GitHub source records roughly 30.3k stars, and Stanford provenance plus inclusion in the Awesome Deep Research index give it a strong research-community signal.
pros: Research design explicitly seeks diverse perspectives before drafting
Academic origin and published evaluation make the approach inspectable
Produces organized long-form reports with citations
cons: Setup requires external model and retrieval services
Wikipedia-style output is not appropriate for every research deliverable
Runs can be slow, costly, and sensitive to retrieval quality

skill.name: claude-deep-research-skill
skill.bestFor: An 8-phase deep-research skill with source-credibility scoring and automated citation/DOI verification for long reports. Smaller/newer, so ranked mid at medium confidence.
skill.rankBasis: Its NVIDIA technical-blog coverage provides credible external validation of the workflow, but its smaller GitHub community keeps it below the established open-source research agents.
pros: Explicit credibility and citation checks address common research-agent failures
Phased workflow is clear enough to audit and customize
Designed to plug into multiple agent harnesses
cons: Independent user-review volume remains limited
A complex workflow increases runtime and model cost
Verification heuristics cannot establish that a source or conclusion is scientifically sound

skill.name: lingzhi227/agent-research-skills
skill.bestFor: A systematic academic literature-review skill running a 6-phase survey from frontier scan to compiled report. Niche and low-star, so ranked last.
skill.rankBasis: The repository documents a complete six-phase literature-survey workflow, but its single GitHub source and low star count indicate limited adoption and independent validation.
pros: Focused workflow is useful for structured literature surveys
Open skill instructions can be inspected and modified
Phased process gives agents clearer checkpoints than a single prompt
cons: Very small community and little independent review evidence
Requires external academic search and model tooling
Maintenance and compatibility risk are higher than for established projects

notes: Skill/Repo track is ranked by real quality, reputation, and adoption — not form factor; kind (skill|repo) is only a tag. last30days (53.4k★, #1 trending) and imbad0202's research pipeline (39.3k★) are the highest-adoption skills; GPT Researcher (28k★) is the recognized de-facto open-source deep-research agent and STORM (30.3k★) is Stanford's report generator — both major repos now ranked alongside the skills on merit. Deep-research/citation skills fill the mid and low tiers. All still depend on an underlying web-search tool and can fabricate citations — verify before citing. No MCP servers. Star counts verified on GitHub. App-track deep-research consensus is stable: OpenAI, Perplexity, Claude, and Gemini lead general research; Elicit/Consensus/NotebookLM lead academic and document-grounded work.
